Well, a few things.
First, we have to place ourselves back in that ancient worldview.
The family, and especially the royal family, was seen as a single unit.
The king wasn't just an individual.
He was the head and representative.
He was the head and representative of his entire house and nation.
His actions had direct, tangible consequences for everyone connected to him.
So it's like, if the captain of a ship drunkenly steers it into an iceberg, the passengers in their cabins go down with the ship, even though they never touched the wheel.
That's a very good analogy.
David, as the captain, steered his family into a moral iceberg.
The death of the child is a direct, tragic result of that collision.
It's a brutal illustration that while God can forgive the guilt of sin,
he doesn't always erase the consequences.
That's a sobering thought, that we can be forgiven, but the damage we cause remains.
It does.

White also suggests that there was a strange kind of mercy in this, in that the child was taken before having to grow up in the violent, dysfunctional house that David's family was about to become.

He contrasts his scene with the great Greek epics like Homer's Iliad.
The story of Achilles and the Trojan War.
Exactly.
In those ancient Greek stories, grief is very predictable.
It's a ritual.
When a hero's friend dies, you know what's going to happen.
He's going to weep, throw dust on his head, refuse to eat for a time.
It's a kind of public performance.
It's a performance of sorrow.
Everyone knows the script.

He's whispering with his servants and David sees them.
He immediately understands.

Then David arose from the earth.

He gets up.
Second, he washed and anointed himself.
Anointing.
That's putting fragrant oil on himself.
It's what you did to freshen up, to prepare for a banquet or an important meeting.
It's the opposite of mourning, which involved dust and ashes.
Third, he changed his apparel, his clothes.
So he takes off the sackcloth, the clothes of mourning, and puts on his clean royal robes.
Fourth, and this is the most stunning one, he came into the house of the Lord and worshiped.
He worshiped.
Let that sink in.
His prayer was just denied in the most painful way possible.
His son is dead, and his first response is to go and worship God.
And finally, fifth, he came back to his own house, and when he required, they set bread before him, and he did eat.
It's what Alter calls estranged behavior.
It's just strange.
It's profoundly weird.
The servants are just staring at him, utterly bewildered.
They can't process it.
They actually have to ask him, what thing is this that you have done?
Their question is so direct.
You did fast and weep for the child while it was alive.

You've got it all backward.
And David's answer, which brings us to part six, is this incredible window into his faith, into his theology.
He calmly explains his logic.
He says, while the child was yet alive, I fasted and wept.
For I said, who can tell whether God will be gracious to me that the child may live?
You see, the fasting and weeping wasn't just an expression of sadness.
It was an act of intercession.
It was work.
It was his strategy for petitioning God.
As long as the child was breathing, there was a possibility, however small, that God might show mercy.
The fight was still on.
But now he is dead, wherefore should I fast?
Can I bring him back again?
And there it is.
A clear-eyed, almost brutally honest acceptance of reality.
The case is closed.
The verdict is final.
The time for fighting is over.

Well, he's dead now, so let's have dinner.

It's an act of profound faith.
It's David saying, I fought as hard as I could.

God brings forth the next link in the royal line.

What would you say are the main takeaways from our look at 2 Samuel chapter 12?
Well, for me, the first one has to be the power of story.
Nathan's approach is a lesson for all time.
He couldn't get through to David with a direct accusation.
He had to use a narrative.
The story of the ewe lamb to bypass his defenses and open his heart.
It really shows that sometimes art and metaphor can communicate truth more effectively than a list of facts.
Facts can be argued with, but a good story just gets inside you.
That's so true.
Second, I think we have to talk about the nature of sin and our blind spots.
That image from Ellen G. White of being wrapped in garments of sin is so powerful.
We can be exactly like David, the rich man in the story.
Harshly judging someone else for a small sin.
While being completely blind to the enormous one in our own life.
It's a humbling thought, isn't it?
It makes you want to ask your closest friends,
Hey, what am I not seeing about myself?
It's a scary but necessary question.

My third takeaway would be the complexity of grief and acceptance.
David's shocking reaction, getting up and worshiping after the child's death,
is a real challenge to our modern ideas about grief.
It suggests that deep faith isn't about God always giving you what you want.
It's about trusting God, even when he doesn't.
That line, I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me, is the key.
That acceptance of reality, coupled with hope for the future,
is what allowed him to move on.
If he had stayed on the dirt floor, consumed by bitterness,
he would have been useless to God, to his family, and to his kingdom.
And finally, that critical distinction between forgiveness and consequences.
Absolutely essential.
David was forgiven, instantly and freely.
God's love for Solomon and for Jedidiah proves that.
But the first child still died.
The violence in his family was still to come.
We have to hold both of those truths together.
Grace is real and it's powerful.
But it doesn't function like a time machine.
It doesn't undo the damage we've done.
It gives us the strength to live through the consequences.
I want to leave our listeners with one last provocative thought to chew on.
We talked about David's servants.
They were whispering in the hall, totally confused.
David wasn't acting the way they expected a grieving person to act.
He wasn't fighting against the reality of death.
He accepted it.
And then he worshipped.
So here's the question for you to think about.
Is it possible that true radical acceptance of reality is itself a form of worship?
That's a powerful idea.
When we stop fighting what has already happened,
when we stop banging our fists on a door that God has clearly closed,
and instead we turn around, wash our faces,
and reengage with the life that is still in front of us,
is that maybe one of the highest acts of faith we can perform?

It's a way of saying,

I don't like this.
